content: Hyponatremia is serum sodium <135 mEq/L, the most common electrolyte disorder in clinical practice. Classification by tonicity: hypotonic (true hyponatremia, most common), isotonic (pseudohyponatremia from hyperlipidemia/hyperproteinemia), hypertonic (hyperglycemia, mannitol). Hypotonic hyponatremia classified by volume status: (1) Hypovolemic - sodium and water loss (diuretics, GI losses, third-spacing, mineralocorticoid deficiency); (2) Euvolemic - normal total body sodium with excess water (SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, psychogenic polydipsia, thiazides); (3) Hypervolemic - increased total body sodium with greater water retention (heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure). Symptoms depend on severity and acuity: mild (nausea, malaise), moderate (headache, confusion, lethargy), severe (seizures, coma, respiratory arrest). Acute (<48 hours) more symptomatic than chronic due to cerebral edema.
